verses.text: Jehoram of Israel does evil, though less than his parents, putting away Baal's image yet clinging to Jeroboam's golden calves. He reforms a little but not at the root. Half-repentance that drops the grosser sin while keeping the favourite one is no true return to God.

Jehoram of Israel does evil, though less than his parents, putting away Baal's image yet clinging to Jeroboam's golden calves. He reforms a little but not at the root. Half-repentance that drops the grosser sin while keeping the favourite one is no true return to God.

Jehoram of Israel does evil, though less than his parents, putting away Baal's image yet clinging to Jeroboam's golden calves. He reforms a little but not at the root. Half-repentance that drops the grosser sin while keeping the favourite one is no true return to God.

When Moab rebels, Jehoram enlists Jehoshaphat and the king of Edom for a joint campaign. Once more the godly Jehoshaphat yokes himself to an evil king's war. Old entanglements are easily resumed; a habit of unwise alliances is hard to break.

When Moab rebels, Jehoram enlists Jehoshaphat and the king of Edom for a joint campaign. Once more the godly Jehoshaphat yokes himself to an evil king's war. Old entanglements are easily resumed; a habit of unwise alliances is hard to break.

After seven days the armies have no water and face ruin, and Jehoshaphat asks for a prophet; Elisha is near. In the crisis they finally seek God — too late to have avoided it, but not too late for mercy. Trouble often drives men to the LORD they ignored in ease.

After seven days the armies have no water and face ruin, and Jehoshaphat asks for a prophet; Elisha is near. In the crisis they finally seek God — too late to have avoided it, but not too late for mercy. Trouble often drives men to the LORD they ignored in ease.

Elisha tells Jehoram to consult his parents' prophets, but for Jehoshaphat's sake he will help, and calls for a minstrel to still his spirit. God's servant honours the one godly man present. And he seeks calm and worship before he speaks — prophecy flows from a quieted heart.

Elisha promises the valley will fill with water though there is no wind or rain, and at morning sacrifice it comes flooding from Edom. God provides without the usual means. He is not bound to clouds and storms; He can pour out blessing by ways we cannot foresee.

The Moabites, seeing the water red as blood in the dawn, think the kings have slain each other and rush to plunder — straight into slaughter. Their false reading of the sign destroys them. The deceived enemy runs eagerly to its own ruin.

The Moabites, seeing the water red as blood in the dawn, think the kings have slain each other and rush to plunder — straight into slaughter. Their false reading of the sign destroys them. The deceived enemy runs eagerly to its own ruin.

In desperation the king of Moab sacrifices his own son on the wall, and a great indignation falls, so Israel withdraws. The scene is dark and strange. Even a clear God-given victory can end in sober retreat; not every triumph is followed to its full end.
